params.text:
Supporting people and colleagues

Inclusive practice means thinking about everyone around you, not only the people you directly support. A colleague may have dyslexia too, and the same understanding and adjustments that help someone using your service can help a member of the team. Whether it is a person you care for or a co-worker, the principles are the same: understand, adapt and treat people with respect.

  • People you support: adapt communication and offer reasonable adjustments as part of everyday care.
  • Colleagues: be considerate about written handovers, rotas and instructions.
  • Shared principles: understanding and respect apply to everyone.

Person-centred approaches

There is no single right way to support someone with dyslexia, because every person is different. A person-centred approach means asking the individual what helps them, listening to their preferences, and shaping support around their needs rather than applying a fixed formula. The person themselves is the expert in what works for them.

This might mean using audio instead of written notes for one person, coloured paper for another, or simply more time and a patient manner. By involving the person in decisions, you offer support that genuinely fits and that respects their independence and choice.

params.question: Reasonable adjustments and understanding for dyslexia are only relevant for the people you support, never for colleagues.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Not quite. Colleagues can have dyslexia as well, so understanding and adjustments matter for the whole team.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. Colleagues may have dyslexia too. Inclusive practice means considering everyone, including members of your team.
